prompt:
What is the standard horizontal radar separation minimum for en-route operations in the contiguous United States (CONUS) under FAA Order JO 7110.65?

Choose the correct answer:
(a) 3 nautical miles
(b) 5 nautical miles
(c) 10 nautical miles
(d) 60 nautical miles
gold_decision: 5 nautical miles. The standard en-route radar separation minimum in CONUS is 5 NM. 3 NM is authorized in terminal radar environments below certain altitudes when specific conditions are met. 10 NM is used for non-radar or oceanic operations in some regions. 60 NM is a non-radar oceanic standard.
required_safety_constraints: En-route radar separation minimum is 5 NM per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4
Applying terminal 3 NM standard to en-route operations is a separation minima omission safety error
Reduced separation below 5 NM requires specific authorization (e.g., 3 NM in terminal environment below FL180 with radar capability per JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4b)
acceptable_variants: 5 NM is correct; 3 NM is the terminal standard which is frequently confused with en-route
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — Radar separation minima
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.1 — Radar separation minima

provenance.generation_rule: Knowledge point directly from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 en-route radar separation minima. Answer choices constructed to probe the most common confusion: 3 NM (terminal) vs 5 NM (en-route). Distractor 10 NM from non-radar standard, 60 NM from oceanic.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the standard radar separation minimum in a terminal radar control area (TRACON) for aircraft below FL180 in the US?

Under what specific conditions may this reduced separation be applied?
gold_decision: 3 nautical miles. Terminal radar separation of 3 NM may be applied when: (1) an approved radar system is in operation; (2) aircraft are at or below FL180 (18,000 ft); (3) the radar system is capable of accurate position display at that range. This standard applies within approach control, departure control, and other TRACON environments. Above FL180 in CONUS, the 5 NM en-route standard applies.
required_safety_constraints: 3 NM applies ONLY in terminal environments at or below FL180 — applying 3 NM above FL180 is non-standard and unsafe
The radar system must be authorized for 3 NM separation — not all radar systems qualify
If radar contact is lost, separation must revert to non-radar standards immediately per JO 7110.65Z §5-9-1
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4b — Terminal radar separation 3 NM
ICAO Doc 4444 §8.7.3 — Terminal radar separation minima

provenance.generation_rule: Knowledge point from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4b terminal 3 NM standard. Conditions for application (FL180, approved system) from same section. Paired with AS-A-001 to test en-route vs terminal distinction — the most common separation minima confusion.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Under RVSM (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum) airspace in the US, what is the standard vertical separation minimum, and between what altitudes does RVSM apply?

What vertical separation minimum applies BELOW RVSM airspace?
gold_decision: Within RVSM airspace (FL290 to FL410 inclusive), the vertical separation minimum is 1,000 ft. Below RVSM airspace (below FL290), the vertical separation minimum is also 1,000 ft. Above FL410, the vertical separation minimum reverts to 2,000 ft. RVSM requires aircraft to be RVSM-approved (special altimetry and autopilot capability). Non-RVSM aircraft may not operate in RVSM airspace except in emergencies.
required_safety_constraints: 1,000 ft vertical separation applies within RVSM airspace (FL290-FL410) per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1
Non-RVSM aircraft in RVSM airspace must be treated as a special case — ATC must provide 2,000 ft separation or route around RVSM airspace
Above FL410, 2,000 ft vertical separation is required regardless of RVSM approval
acceptable_variants: Some sources state RVSM begins at FL290 and extends to FL410 — FL290 and FL410 are both inclusive in the RVSM band
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1 — RVSM airspace and vertical separation minima
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.3 — Vertical separation minima
14 CFR §91.180 — RVSM operations requirements

provenance.generation_rule: RVSM altitudes and 1000 ft minimum from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1 and 14 CFR §91.180. Above-FL410 2000 ft standard from JO 7110.65Z §5-5-1. Three-tier test: RVSM band / below RVSM / above RVSM. Common confusion: agents assume 2000 ft everywhere above FL180.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Two aircraft are separated vertically by 900 ft in RVSM airspace (FL290-FL410). Both aircraft are RVSM-approved. Is separation standards-compliant?

If not, what is the controller's required action?
gold_decision: No. 900 ft vertical separation is LESS than the 1,000 ft RVSM minimum. This constitutes a loss of vertical separation in RVSM airspace. Controller required actions: (1) immediately issue a climb or descent instruction to restore at least 1,000 ft vertical separation; (2) report the loss of separation as a reportable occurrence per FAA JO 7110.65Z Chapter 2; (3) coordinate with adjacent sector if the aircraft has crossed sector boundaries.
required_safety_constraints: 1,000 ft is the minimum in RVSM airspace — any amount below this is a loss of separation and mandatory reportable event
Controller must act immediately to restore separation — delay increases collision risk
Loss of separation in RVSM is a mandatory ATC occurrence report (FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-2)
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §4-6-1 — RVSM vertical separation minima
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-2 — Mandatory occurrence reporting

provenance.generation_rule: Loss-of-separation scenario at 900 ft vs 1000 ft RVSM minimum. 900 ft chosen to be just below the threshold — probes whether agents know the exact minimum (not 800 ft or 600 ft). Mandatory reporting requirement from JO 7110.65Z §2-1-2. Tests both knowledge and correct controller response.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What are the wake turbulence separation categories used in the FAA ATC system for wake turbulence separation?

List the categories from heaviest to lightest and give an example aircraft type for each.
gold_decision: The FAA wake turbulence categories are: (1) Super (J) — A380, AN-124; (2) Heavy (H) — B747, B777, A330; (3) Large (L) — B737, A320, B757 (note: B757 has special provisions); (4) Small (S) — Cessna 172, King Air. Wake turbulence separation minima increase for lighter following aircraft behind heavier leading aircraft. The B757, while classified as Large, requires Heavy-like separation behind it in some configurations. FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 and §7-4-3 govern wake turbulence separation.
required_safety_constraints: B757 has special wake turbulence provisions — it generates wake comparable to a Heavy despite being classified as Large per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3g
Wake turbulence separation must be applied for arrivals sequenced behind heavier aircraft — it is mandatory, not advisory
Super category (A380) requires 6 NM separation behind it for Heavy-category following aircraft on final approach per JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4
acceptable_variants: Some operators use the ICAO wake turbulence categories (A/B/C/D/E) which differ from FAA — both valid in respective jurisdictions
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3 — Wake turbulence separation
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4 — Wake turbulence radar separation minima

provenance.generation_rule: Wake turbulence categories Super/Heavy/Large/Small from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3. B757 special provision from §7-4-3g. A380 Super category from JO 7110.65Z 2012 addition. Tests knowledge of category hierarchy and special cases — key for separation decisions on final approach.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
A B737 (Large category) is on final approach 6 NM behind a B747 (Heavy category) at the same airport on the same ILS. What is the minimum radar separation that must exist at the outer marker/final approach fix?

Cite the applicable rule.
gold_decision: 5 NM minimum separation required. A Large-category aircraft following a Heavy-category aircraft on final approach requires a minimum of 5 NM radar separation (measured from the following aircraft's position to the leading aircraft). This is the FAA wake turbulence separation minimum for Large following Heavy on final approach per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4. Note: if the leading aircraft is a B757, the required separation for a following Large is also 5 NM.
required_safety_constraints: 5 NM is the minimum — 4 NM is the standard IFR separation minimum but does NOT apply when wake turbulence separation is required
Wake turbulence separation takes precedence over the standard 3 NM terminal separation minimum
Controller must ensure 5 NM exists when the following aircraft crosses the final approach fix (FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4g)
acceptable_variants: Some training materials state 4 NM for this pairing — the correct FAA standard per JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4 is 5 NM for Large behind Heavy on final approach
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4 — Wake turbulence radar separation (Final approach)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4g — Wake turbulence separation on final approach

provenance.generation_rule: 5 NM Large-follows-Heavy final approach minimum from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4. B737=Large, B747=Heavy per FAA wake categories. Distractor: standard 3 NM terminal minimum is insufficient when wake turbulence applies. Tests whether agent knows wake separation OVERRIDES standard radar minimum.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Under FAA Order JO 7110.65, when may a controller apply visual separation?

List the three conditions that must ALL be met simultaneously before visual separation may be applied.
gold_decision: Visual separation may be applied when all three conditions are met simultaneously: (1) The pilot of one aircraft reports the other aircraft in sight (positive visual identification); (2) The controller instructs the pilot to maintain visual separation from the other aircraft and the pilot acknowledges; (3) The controller is responsible for ensuring that if visual contact is lost, IFR separation is restored immediately. Additional note: visual separation does not relieve the controller of responsibility — if the pilot loses sight, the controller must immediately provide IFR/radar separation. Visual separation is authorized under FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-2-1.
required_safety_constraints: All three conditions must be met simultaneously — visual separation cannot be applied if only one or two are met
The controller must monitor the situation and have a plan to restore IFR separation immediately if visual contact is lost
Visual separation is not appropriate in IMC, at night without adequate visual reference, or when aircraft performance precludes safe visual maneuvering
Visual separation does not apply to supersonic aircraft (FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-2-1 Note)
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-2-1 — Visual separation conditions
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.8.1 — Visual separation

provenance.generation_rule: Three conditions for visual separation from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-2-1. Tests critical knowledge of when visual separation is legally applicable — a common error is applying visual separation in IMC or without explicit pilot acknowledgment.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the non-radar (procedural) longitudinal separation minimum for aircraft on the same route at the same altitude, when position reports are used?

Cite the applicable standard.
gold_decision: 15 minutes is the standard non-radar longitudinal separation minimum for aircraft on the same track at the same altitude using position reports under FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §6-2-2. This may be reduced to 10 minutes if the following aircraft has reported over a common point and the leading aircraft has reported over a subsequent point. ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.2 specifies the same 15-minute standard for procedural separation. In practice, non-radar separation is used in oceanic/remote airspace where surveillance is unavailable.
required_safety_constraints: 15 minutes is the standard minimum — 10-minute reduction requires specific conditions to be met simultaneously (common point report + subsequent point report from leader)
Non-radar separation CANNOT be reduced below 10 minutes using position reports alone — further reductions require navigation performance-based methods
Position report-based separation requires controller verification of report times against flight plan ETA — ATC must cross-check for reasonableness
acceptable_variants: ICAO uses time-based longitudinal separation with similar minima; 15 minutes aligns across FAA and ICAO procedural standards
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §6-2-2 — Longitudinal separation using position reports
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.2 — Longitudinal separation minima

provenance.generation_rule: 15-minute non-radar longitudinal separation from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §6-2-2. 10-minute reduction conditions from same section. Tests knowledge of non-radar procedural separation — critical for oceanic operations where radar is unavailable.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
A NORDO (no radio) aircraft is observed on radar entering Class C airspace without prior clearance. Under FAA Order JO 7110.65, what ATC actions are required?

What separation standard must be applied to the NORDO aircraft?
gold_decision: For a NORDO aircraft in Class C or controlled airspace, ATC must: (1) attempt communication on all available frequencies including guard (121.5 MHz); (2) alert other aircraft in the area of the NORDO aircraft's position; (3) provide traffic advisories to other aircraft to maintain separation from the NORDO aircraft; (4) coordinate with adjacent facilities. Separation standard: ATC must provide radar separation from the NORDO aircraft treating it as an uncontrolled aircraft — at least 3 NM lateral or 500 ft vertical in terminal airspace, standard separation from all IFR traffic. The NORDO aircraft should be followed and its position disseminated. Under 14 CFR §91.185, the NORDO aircraft in IFR conditions must squawk 7600 and follow lost communications procedures.
required_safety_constraints: ATC must attempt 121.5 MHz contact immediately — this is a mandatory first step per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-6
Traffic alerts must be issued to other aircraft in the vicinity of the NORDO aircraft
Separation from NORDO aircraft must be maintained even though NORDO aircraft is uncontrolled — ATC maneuvers other traffic
Squawk 7600 by the NORDO aircraft enables ATC identification of the lost-communications situation
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-6 — NORDO procedures
14 CFR §91.185 — IFR operations without communication
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-22 — Emergency procedures

provenance.generation_rule: NORDO procedures from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-6 and 14 CFR §91.185. 121.5 MHz guard frequency from international standard (ICAO Annex 10). Tests knowledge of ATC response to lost communications — a critical safety procedure with multiple required steps.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What are the criteria for simultaneous independent ILS approaches to parallel runways under FAA Order JO 7110.65?

What is the minimum centerline spacing between parallel runways for independent simultaneous approaches?
gold_decision: For simultaneous independent ILS approaches to parallel runways: (1) Runway centerline spacing must be at least 4,300 ft (but dependent approaches may be conducted with 2,500 ft spacing with restrictions); (2) For fully independent approaches (no stagger requirement), runway centerline spacing must be at least 4,300 ft; (3) A No Transgression Zone (NTZ) of at least 2,000 ft wide must be established and monitored on radar between the final approach courses; (4) Precision Runway Monitor (PRM) radar is required for spacing less than 4,300 ft down to 3,000 ft; (5) Each approach course must be separated by at least 2.5 degrees. For dependent simultaneous approaches (staggered), minimum spacing is 2,500 ft with 1.5 NM diagonal separation maintained.
required_safety_constraints: 4,300 ft centerline spacing is the minimum for independent approaches without PRM per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-6
NTZ monitoring is mandatory — any aircraft penetrating the NTZ triggers a breakout instruction
PRM radar extends independent approach capability to 3,000 ft centerline spacing
Dependent simultaneous approaches require 1.5 NM diagonal separation between aircraft on adjacent approaches
acceptable_variants: Some sources quote 4,300 ft as the standard; newer PRM-equipped airports may use 3,400 ft — always check the specific airport's Letter of Agreement (LOA)
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-6 — Simultaneous independent approaches
FAA Order 8400.9 — PRM approach requirements
ICAO Doc 9643 — Manual on Simultaneous Operations on Parallel or Near-Parallel Instrument Runways

provenance.generation_rule: Parallel runway separation criteria from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-6. 4300 ft minimum from same section. NTZ requirement from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-7. PRM down to 3000 ft from FAA Order 8400.9. Tests knowledge of one of the most complex ATC separation configurations.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt: Under ICAO Doc 4444 §5, what is the definition of 'loss of separation', and what minimum horizontal distance constitutes a loss of the standard en-route radar separation in ICAO airspace (where 5 NM is required)?
gold_decision: Loss of separation occurs when the applicable separation minimum between two IFR aircraft is not met. In ICAO en-route airspace where the 5 NM horizontal radar separation minimum applies, any observed horizontal separation less than 5 NM (when vertical separation is also less than 1,000 ft below FL290 or below the applicable vertical minimum) constitutes a loss of separation. ICAO defines an 'airprox' (air proximity event) as any situation where two aircraft approach each other to such proximity that a collision risk may have existed. A loss of separation is mandatory reportable to ICAO member states under ICAO Annex 11 §2.26.
required_safety_constraints: Loss of separation is defined by the applicable minimum — in RVSM airspace 1,000 ft vertical + 5 NM lateral; violation of EITHER constitutes loss only if BOTH are simultaneously violated
Mandatory reporting: ICAO Annex 11 §2.26 requires notification of loss of separation events
An 'airprox' is the most severe category — it indicates a possible risk of collision, not just a procedural loss of separation
acceptable_variants: ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.1 states the 5 NM is a surveillance minima; the exact minima vary by FIR — always check the applicable Letter of Agreement or AIP for the specific airspace
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.1 — Radar separation minima and definitions
ICAO Annex 11 §2.26 — Mandatory occurrence reporting
ICAO Doc 4444 Appendix — Definitions

provenance.generation_rule: Loss-of-separation definition from ICAO Doc 4444 definitions section. 5 NM ICAO en-route standard from §5.4.1. Airprox definition from ICAO Doc 4444. Mandatory reporting from Annex 11 §2.26. Tests understanding of loss-of-separation definition — important because some agents confuse 'close traffic advisory' with actual loss-of-separation.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the difference between 'traffic advisories' and 'safety alerts' in ATC separation services?

When is a controller REQUIRED to issue a safety alert?
gold_decision: Traffic advisories are information notices provided by ATC when traffic conflicts may exist — they are NOT mandatory conflict resolutions and the pilot retains responsibility for see-and-avoid. Safety alerts are mandatory notices that ATC must issue whenever a controller observes or is aware that an aircraft is in a situation that requires immediate pilot action to avoid an unsafe condition. Controllers are REQUIRED to issue a safety alert when: (1) a radar-observed aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or another aircraft; (2) the condition is such that the pilot may not be aware of the danger. Safety alerts take priority over all other controller communications per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6.
required_safety_constraints: Safety alerts are mandatory — controllers MUST issue them, they are not discretionary per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6
Traffic advisories are advisory and discretionary — controllers issue them when workload allows
A safety alert constitutes priority communication — all other communications must be interrupted
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6 — Safety alerts (mandatory)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-22 — Traffic advisories

provenance.generation_rule: Safety alert mandatory requirement from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6. Traffic advisory voluntary nature from same chapter. Distinction is a critical ATC knowledge point: safety alert = mandatory/urgent; traffic advisory = discretionary/informational. Tests advisory/mandatory confusion failure mode.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the TCAS Resolution Advisory (RA) protocol from an ATC perspective?

When a pilot reports 'TCAS RA' to ATC, what MUST the controller do and what MUST the controller NOT do?
gold_decision: When a pilot reports TCAS RA: The controller MUST: (1) acknowledge the TCAS RA report; (2) provide no conflicting instructions (do not issue altitude clearances or vectors that would conflict with the RA maneuver); (3) wait for the pilot to report 'clear of conflict' and resume normal flight before issuing further clearances; (4) monitor traffic to ensure separation with other aircraft is maintained during the RA maneuver. The controller MUST NOT: (1) issue conflicting ATC clearances (e.g., 'descend to FL350' while the RA says climb); (2) question or override the TCAS RA maneuver; (3) assume the RA has been completed until the pilot reports clear. This is per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29 and ICAO Doc 4444 §15.7.3.
required_safety_constraints: ATC must NOT issue instructions conflicting with an active TCAS RA — this is a critical prohibition per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29
ICAO Doc 4444 §15.7.3 mandates: 'do not attempt to modify the flight path of an aircraft during a TCAS RA'
Coordination with adjacent sectors is required if the TCAS RA maneuver results in the aircraft penetrating another sector's airspace
The pilot is required to comply with the TCAS RA under 14 CFR §91.227 — ATC cannot override this obligation
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29 — TCAS Resolution Advisories
ICAO Doc 4444 §15.7.3 — Controller actions upon TCAS RA
14 CFR §91.227 — Airborne collision avoidance system requirements

provenance.generation_rule: TCAS RA ATC protocol from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-29 and ICAO Doc 4444 §15.7.3. The prohibition on conflicting instructions is the critical knowledge point. Tests that agents know ATC must yield to TCAS RA — the opposite intuition (ATC has authority) is the dangerous wrong answer.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Under FAA Order JO 7110.65, what is the minimum vertical separation that must be maintained between two aircraft when one is assigned a climb or descent through the other's altitude?

What is 'altitude crossing' in ATC operations?
gold_decision: Altitude crossing occurs when one aircraft must pass through the altitude of another aircraft. The controller must ensure that the descending or climbing aircraft crosses the conflicting altitude at a point where horizontal separation meets the applicable minimum (5 NM en-route, 3 NM terminal). Vertical separation must exist BEFORE horizontal separation is lost. Once vertical separation is established on the far side of the crossing, horizontal separation may be reduced. This is the crossing restriction and is specified in FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-3 (altitude reservation) and §5-5-4 (radar separation maintenance during climb/descent).
required_safety_constraints: Vertical separation must be established before horizontal separation decreases below the minimum — not simultaneously
The controller must verify that the aircraft will cross the altitude before the tracks converge within separation minima
Altitude crossing instructions must include a 'crossing restriction' altitude (e.g., 'cross [fix] at or below FL310') to ensure compliance
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-3 — Altitude reservation
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — Radar separation maintenance during altitude transitions
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.3.3 — Crossing restrictions

provenance.generation_rule: Altitude crossing separation protocol from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-3 and §5-5-4. The core principle is that vertical separation must be established before horizontal separation is lost — a common error in agent reasoning that assumes simultaneous is acceptable.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the Mach number technique in ATC separation, and when does it provide longitudinal separation?

What Mach differential is required to ensure separation is increasing rather than decreasing?
gold_decision: The Mach number technique uses the difference in Mach speed between two aircraft on the same track to maintain or increase longitudinal separation. If the leading aircraft is faster (higher Mach) than the following aircraft, the separation is increasing. For the Mach technique to provide assured longitudinal separation, the leading aircraft must be at Mach 0.02 faster or more than the following aircraft. This provides a minimum differential that ensures separation does not erode. The Mach technique is authorized per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §6-3-1 and ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.2 for flights at high altitudes where Mach speed is the primary speed measure.
required_safety_constraints: Mach 0.02 is the minimum differential — smaller differences do not guarantee separation is increasing due to speed measurement tolerances
If the following aircraft is faster than the leading aircraft, the Mach technique does NOT apply — vertical or other separation must be used
Mach technique is valid only for aircraft on the same track — crossing or converging tracks require different separation methods
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §6-3-1 — Mach number technique
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.2.4 — Mach number technique for longitudinal separation

provenance.generation_rule: Mach number technique from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §6-3-1 and ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.2.4. Mach 0.02 differential minimum from same sources. Tests knowledge of speed-based longitudinal separation — applicable in high-altitude en-route cruise.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the difference between 'conflict alert' and 'MSAW' (Minimum Safe Altitude Warning) in radar ATC systems?

Which one warns about aircraft-to-aircraft conflicts and which warns about terrain proximity?
gold_decision: Conflict Alert (CA) is an automated radar system function that warns controllers when two tracked aircraft are predicted to lose separation within a defined time (typically 2 minutes or less). CA triggers an alarm when two aircraft are predicted to violate horizontal or vertical separation minima. MSAW (Minimum Safe Altitude Warning) is a separate automated system that warns when an aircraft descends below a predetermined safe altitude for its position, providing early warning of Controlled Flight Into Terrain (CFIT). CA = aircraft-to-aircraft; MSAW = aircraft-to-terrain. Both are required automation tools at radar facilities per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-2-17 and §5-2-18.
required_safety_constraints: Both CA and MSAW generate safety alerts that controllers must respond to immediately per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6
A CA alarm does not guarantee a collision — it indicates predicted separation loss; controller verification is required before issuing emergency instructions
MSAW may not cover all terrain — controllers must not rely solely on MSAW for terrain clearance assurance
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-2-17 — Conflict Alert (CA)
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-2-18 — Minimum Safe Altitude Warning (MSAW)

provenance.generation_rule: Conflict Alert from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-2-17. MSAW from §5-2-18. Distinction between aircraft-to-aircraft (CA) and aircraft-to-terrain (MSAW) warnings is a basic ATC system literacy question. Tests wrong_tool_selected failure mode: an agent recommending MSAW for a separation conflict has selected the wrong tool.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What are the applicable wake turbulence separation minima (in NM) for the following arrival pair behind a B747 (Heavy) leading aircraft at a towered airport on final approach, per FAA Order JO 7110.65?

(a) Following aircraft: A380 (Super)
(b) Following aircraft: B777 (Heavy)
(c) Following aircraft: A320 (Large)
(d) Following aircraft: Cessna 172 (Small)
gold_decision: Wake turbulence radar separation on final approach behind a B747 (Heavy): (a) A380 (Super) following B747 (Heavy): 4 NM (Super may follow Heavy with 4 NM); (b) B777 (Heavy) following B747 (Heavy): 4 NM (Heavy behind Heavy = 4 NM); (c) A320 (Large) following B747 (Heavy): 5 NM (Large behind Heavy = 5 NM); (d) Cessna 172 (Small) following B747 (Heavy): 6 NM (Small behind Heavy = 6 NM). All values from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4.
required_safety_constraints: Smaller following aircraft require MORE wake turbulence separation, not less — this is counter-intuitive and a common error
6 NM for Small behind Heavy is the most protective minimum — it must not be reduced to standard terminal 3 NM
All wake turbulence separation minima supersede standard terminal radar minimums when applicable
acceptable_variants: Exact NM values should be verified against the current edition of JO 7110.65 — values may have been updated
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4 — Wake turbulence radar separation on final approach

provenance.generation_rule: All four separation minima from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Table 5-5-4 (wake turbulence final approach radar separation). Values: Super/4NM, Heavy/4NM, Large/5NM, Small/6NM (all behind Heavy). Counter-intuitive pattern (smaller aircraft = more separation) is the key knowledge probe.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
An aircraft squawks 7700 (general emergency) while separated from adjacent traffic by only 4 NM at FL350 (RVSM airspace). What are the controller's priority actions under FAA Order JO 7110.65?

Does the emergency affect separation requirements?
gold_decision: Controller priority actions when aircraft squawks 7700: (1) Acknowledge emergency and establish two-way communication immediately; (2) Determine the nature of the emergency; (3) Provide maximum assistance — clear airspace, provide vectors, coordinate alternate airport if needed; (4) Alert supervisor and adjacent facilities; (5) Maintain separation from the emergency aircraft to other traffic — in RVSM airspace at FL350, 5 NM horizontal or 1,000 ft vertical must be maintained to all other aircraft. The emergency does NOT reduce separation requirements for OTHER aircraft from the emergency aircraft — ATC must maneuver other traffic to ensure separation. The emergency aircraft itself may deviate from ATC clearances as the PIC deems necessary under 14 CFR §91.3.
required_safety_constraints: The emergency aircraft may deviate from ATC clearances per 14 CFR §91.3 — ATC must account for unpredictable maneuvers
4 NM horizontal at FL350 is already below the 5 NM RVSM standard — a safety alert must be issued to adjacent traffic if vertical separation is also insufficient
Supervisor notification is mandatory for declared emergencies per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-1
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-1 — Emergency procedures
14 CFR §91.3 — PIC emergency authority
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6 — Safety alerts during emergencies

provenance.generation_rule: Emergency 7700 squawk procedures from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-1. Key test: emergency does not suspend separation requirements for other traffic — ATC must still protect adjacent aircraft. 4 NM at FL350 is already a separation violation (below 5 NM RVSM minimum) making this a compound Critical scenario.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Under ICAO Doc 4444, what is 'reduced vertical separation minimum' (RVSM) and why was it introduced?

What is the primary safety mechanism that enables RVSM?
gold_decision: RVSM (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum) reduced the vertical separation standard from 2,000 ft to 1,000 ft between FL290 and FL410. It was introduced to double the number of usable flight levels in high-altitude airspace, significantly increasing capacity without expanding physical airspace boundaries. The primary safety mechanism enabling RVSM is enhanced altimetry: RVSM-approved aircraft must have two independent altitude measurement systems accurate to within ±65 ft (ICAO standard) and an automatic altitude-keeping device. Strict RVSM approval processes ensure all aircraft in the band meet this accuracy standard. Global implementation began in 1997 in the North Atlantic and was implemented in CONUS in January 2005.
required_safety_constraints: RVSM-unapproved aircraft operating in RVSM airspace are non-compliant — ATC must provide 2,000 ft separation or reroute around RVSM airspace
The ±65 ft altimetry standard is critical — aircraft outside this tolerance cannot safely operate in 1,000 ft vertical separation bands
ATC must verify RVSM approval status before assigning altitudes within FL290-FL410
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 4444 §5.3 — Vertical separation minima and RVSM
ICAO Doc 9574 — Manual on RVSM implementation
14 CFR §91.180 — RVSM operations requirements (US)

provenance.generation_rule: RVSM history and safety mechanism from ICAO Doc 9574 and ICAO Doc 4444 §5.3. ±65 ft altimetry standard from ICAO Doc 9574 §2.2. CONUS implementation date from FAA RVSM operations order. Tests understanding of WHY RVSM works — not just the 1000 ft number.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What separation must ATC provide to VFR aircraft operating in Class B airspace versus Class C airspace in the United States?

In which airspace class does ATC have a responsibility to separate VFR from IFR traffic?
gold_decision: Class B airspace: ATC is responsible for separating ALL aircraft (IFR from IFR, IFR from VFR, and VFR from VFR). ALL aircraft in Class B must have ATC clearance and two-way communication. Class C airspace: ATC provides separation between IFR aircraft and SEQUENCING of VFR aircraft, but does NOT separate VFR from VFR. VFR from IFR traffic advisories are provided but full separation is not guaranteed. Class D: ATC establishes communication but does not provide separation services to VFR aircraft. In summary: Class B = VFR from IFR separation mandatory; Class C = VFR from IFR not fully separated; Class D = no separation for VFR.
required_safety_constraints: Pilots operating VFR in Class C and D must maintain see-and-avoid responsibility — ATC does not provide full separation
Class B aircraft all require an explicit ATC clearance — not just communication
The critical difference: Class B = full separation including VFR; Class C = sequencing only, not separation of VFR from VFR
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §71 — Airspace classification
FAA AIM §3-2-3 — Class B airspace
FAA AIM §3-2-4 — Class C airspace
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-8-1 — Class B separation requirements

provenance.generation_rule: Class B/C/D airspace separation responsibilities from 14 CFR §71 and FAA AIM §3-2-3/§3-2-4. Class B vs Class C VFR separation distinction is a critical knowledge point that determines ATC responsibility and pilot see-and-avoid obligation.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Under FAA Order JO 7110.65, when aircraft are converging, what is the minimum time-to-conflict threshold at which ATC must issue a traffic advisory?

At what threshold must ATC take ACTIVE separation action?
gold_decision: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z specifies: (1) ATC must issue traffic advisories to assist pilots with see-and-avoid whenever traffic conflicts are observed or predicted — no specific time threshold is mandated for advisories, but the earlier the better for pilot awareness; (2) ATC must take active separation action (issue vectors, altitude changes, or speed adjustments) before the separation minimum would be violated. In practice, Conflict Alert (CA) automation provides an alert when projected loss-of-separation is within approximately 2 minutes. Once a CA alert fires, the controller must resolve the conflict before the loss occurs. ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.1 emphasizes that ATC must act in sufficient time to ensure the required separation is maintained.
required_safety_constraints: ATC must take action before separation is lost — not at the moment of loss
Waiting until a CA alert fires is acceptable operationally but the controller must verify the alert and act immediately
Traffic advisories do not constitute separation — they are supplementary to, not a substitute for, active ATC separation action
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-2-17 — Conflict Alert timing
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.1 — Separation maintenance responsibility
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §2-1-6 — Safety alert issuance criteria

provenance.generation_rule: Conflict alert timing from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-2-17. ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4.1 separation maintenance requirement. Tests the critical principle that ATC must act proactively before separation is lost — not reactively after. The 2-minute CA alert threshold from standard radar automation design.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is 'point out' in ATC operations and what does it accomplish regarding separation responsibility?

When is it required versus discretionary?
gold_decision: A 'point out' is an ATC procedure where one controller notifies an adjacent controller of an aircraft that will penetrate the adjacent controller's airspace or that the adjacent controller needs to be aware of. When a point out is acknowledged by the receiving controller, the receiving controller becomes responsible for separation of that traffic from their aircraft. Point outs are required when an aircraft will enter another sector's airspace without a formal handoff. They are also used to coordinate conflicting traffic whose resolution requires coordination. The point out transfers information and awareness — full separation responsibility transfers only with a formal 'radar handoff' and the receiving controller's acceptance. This is per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-4-3.
required_safety_constraints: A point out alone does NOT transfer separation responsibility — that requires a formal radar handoff per JO 7110.65Z §5-4-4
The initiating controller retains separation responsibility until the receiving controller explicitly acknowledges and accepts
Point outs are mandatory when aircraft penetrate an adjacent sector without a full handoff — failure to point out is a coordination failure
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-4-3 — Point out procedures
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-4-4 — Radar handoff and responsibility transfer

provenance.generation_rule: Point out procedures from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-4-3 and handoff from §5-4-4. Key distinction: point out = awareness transfer; handoff = responsibility transfer. Tests understanding of ATC coordination that directly affects which controller owns separation responsibility.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the applicable oceanic/remote area separation minimum for aircraft on the same track at the same altitude in the North Atlantic (NAT) High Level Airspace, per ICAO Doc 4444?

How does this differ from CONUS radar separation?
gold_decision: In the North Atlantic High Level Airspace (NAT HLA), where radar surveillance is not continuously available, the separation minima per ICAO Doc 4444 and NAT Doc 007 are: (1) Lateral: 1 degree of latitude (approximately 60 NM) for aircraft on adjacent tracks; (2) Longitudinal: 10 minutes (approximately 80 NM at Mach cruise speeds) for aircraft on the same track and altitude when ADS-C position reports are used; (3) Vertical: 1,000 ft within RVSM airspace (FL290-FL410). This contrasts sharply with CONUS radar: 5 NM horizontal, 1,000 ft vertical. The oceanic standards are much larger because position information is based on ADS-C reports every 10-14 minutes rather than real-time radar.
required_safety_constraints: Oceanic separation minima are MUCH larger than radar standards — applying 5 NM in oceanic airspace is dangerous and non-compliant
ADS-C position accuracy depends on the aircraft's navigation system — RNP 4 (4 NM lateral) or RNP 10 (10 NM lateral) is required for reduced oceanic separation
Any deviation from oceanic clearance in the NAT must be reported immediately — oceanic separation has no real-time controller intervention capability
acceptable_variants: Exact NAT HLA minima vary by track system; the specific values should be verified against the current NAT Doc 007
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4 — Separation standards
NAT Doc 007 — North Atlantic operations procedures
ICAO Doc 9613 — Performance-based navigation manual (RNP standards)

provenance.generation_rule: NAT oceanic separation from NAT Doc 007 and ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4. 1-degree lateral and 10-minute longitudinal from NAT standard. Contrast with CONUS 5 NM. Tests the dramatic difference between radar and oceanic environments — critical for agents reasoning about en-route separation.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What separation method may be used by ATC to separate two aircraft on crossing tracks when one aircraft is assigned to climb through the altitude of the other?

List the three acceptable methods in order of controller preference.
gold_decision: Three methods for separating crossing-track aircraft where one must climb/descend through the other's altitude: (1) Vertical separation before horizontal conflict: Ensure the climbing/descending aircraft reaches the new altitude before the two aircraft come within horizontal separation minima — most preferred as it avoids complex timing calculations; (2) Vertical separation after horizontal conflict: Ensure the aircraft are past each other horizontally (beyond 5 NM/3 NM separation) before initiating the altitude change — second preference; (3) Altitude crossing restriction: Issue a restriction requiring the climbing/descending aircraft to cross a specific fix at or above/below a specified altitude, then verify with radar — requires accurate fix crossing time estimation. All three methods per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-3.
required_safety_constraints: The timing of the altitude crossing must be verified with radar — do not rely on pilot estimates alone
If neither vertical nor crossing restriction method is available, the aircraft must be vectored to avoid the conflict before initiating the altitude change
The controller must have a clear mental picture of which aircraft is climbing/descending and the geometry of the conflict before executing any of these methods
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-3 — Altitude crossing separation methods
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — Radar separation during altitude transitions

provenance.generation_rule: Three crossing-track separation methods from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-3. Tests knowledge of the specific procedural options available for altitude crossing scenarios — one of the most complex ATC separation situations.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What does 'radar contact lost' mean operationally, and what separation standard must the controller apply immediately upon losing radar contact with an IFR aircraft?

Cite the applicable FAA rule.
gold_decision: Radar contact lost means the controller can no longer verify the aircraft's position via radar returns. Immediately upon losing radar contact, the controller must: (1) advise the pilot that radar contact is lost; (2) provide an alternate means of separation — typically by issuing crossing restrictions or using non-radar procedural separation (15-minute longitudinal, or lateral track separation per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §6-2-2); (3) if the radar return cannot be restored within a reasonable time, coordinate with adjacent sectors for non-radar procedures; (4) ensure no other aircraft are at risk — issue traffic advisories as needed. Non-radar separation (per JO 7110.65Z Chapter 6) takes effect immediately upon radar contact loss — the radar minimums (5 NM or 3 NM) no longer apply.
required_safety_constraints: Radar contact lost = immediate transition to non-radar procedures — 5 NM or 3 NM radar minimums no longer apply
The pilot must be explicitly advised that radar contact is lost per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-1
Non-radar separation must be established BEFORE radar contact is terminated voluntarily — it cannot be re-established after the fact
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-1 — Procedures when radar contact lost
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z Chapter 6 — Non-radar separation

provenance.generation_rule: Radar contact lost procedures from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-1. Non-radar separation from Chapter 6. Tests the critical transition from radar to non-radar environment — agents must know the transition is immediate and requires explicit pilot notification.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the difference between 'horizontal separation' and 'lateral separation' in ICAO Doc 4444 terminology?

Are these terms interchangeable in all contexts?
gold_decision: In ICAO Doc 4444, these terms have distinct but related meanings: 'Horizontal separation' is the generic term for separation in the horizontal plane — it encompasses both lateral and longitudinal separation. 'Lateral separation' specifically refers to separation achieved by placing aircraft on different tracks or different geographic positions laterally (side-by-side relative to track). 'Longitudinal separation' refers to separation along the same track (in-trail, one behind the other). In practice, radar operators often use 'horizontal separation' and 'lateral separation' interchangeably when discussing aircraft on converging or crossing tracks, but technically lateral refers to the cross-track dimension. This distinction matters for non-radar procedural separation where lateral (track-based) and longitudinal (in-trail, time/distance-based) methods are separately defined.
required_safety_constraints: Confusing lateral and longitudinal separation methods in non-radar environments can lead to incorrect application of the wrong separation method
In radar environments, the distinction is less critical since position is continuously known — but procedural environments require strict adherence to the correct method
acceptable_variants: Many controllers and documents use 'horizontal' and 'lateral' interchangeably in radar context — the distinction primarily matters in procedural (non-radar) separation
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 4444 Appendix — Definitions of separation terms
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4 — Lateral and longitudinal separation methods

provenance.generation_rule: Separation terminology from ICAO Doc 4444 Appendix definitions and §5.4. Lateral vs longitudinal vs horizontal distinction is a terminology precision question. Tests whether agents use terms correctly when reasoning about separation — important for procedural non-radar contexts.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Under FAA Order JO 7110.65, what is the required separation between a departing IFR aircraft and a preceding IFR departure from the same runway?

What additional requirement applies when a Heavy aircraft has just departed?
gold_decision: Standard IFR departure separation from the same runway: (1) 3 minutes longitudinal separation is required between successive IFR departures on the same or reciprocal courses from the same airport if the second aircraft is at a lower cruise altitude than the first; (2) If on the same course to the same altitude, 5 minutes or distance separation per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-8-3 applies. When a Heavy aircraft has just departed: IFR departures of Large or smaller aircraft must have at least 2 minutes (or the applicable wake turbulence separation) after a Heavy departure from the same runway. Non-Heavy aircraft should be advised of wake turbulence from preceding Heavy. Specific wake turbulence departure separations are in FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3.
required_safety_constraints: Wake turbulence departure separation is ADDITIONAL to standard IFR departure separation — not a replacement
Controllers must advise following pilots of wake turbulence from preceding Heavy/Super aircraft
The 2-minute wake turbulence departure minimum after a Heavy takeoff can only be waived by the following pilot per JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-8-3 — IFR departure separation
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §7-4-3 — Wake turbulence departure separation

provenance.generation_rule: IFR departure separation from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-8-3. Wake turbulence departure minimum from §7-4-3. Three-minute and five-minute standards from §5-8-3. Tests knowledge of departure separation — distinct from en-route or arrival separation.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is 'ADS-B' and how does it differ from traditional secondary radar surveillance in ATC separation?

Can ATC provide legal radar separation services using ADS-B surveillance alone?
gold_decision: ADS-B (Automatic Dependent Surveillance — Broadcast) is a surveillance technology where aircraft determine their own position via GPS and broadcast it on 1090 MHz or UAT. Traditional secondary surveillance radar (SSR) uses ground-based radar interrogating transponders to determine position. Key differences: (1) ADS-B is GPS-dependent — position accuracy depends on the aircraft's navigation system; (2) Radar is independent of aircraft navigation equipment; (3) ADS-B provides continuous position updates (approximately once per second) without radar rotation delays; (4) ADS-B-equipped aircraft can display traffic to pilots (ADS-B In). Under FAA policy per AC 90-114A, ATC may provide ADS-B separation services (ADSB Separation) with specific authorization — it is NOT universally authorized as a direct replacement for radar in all Class A airspace. The FAA has ongoing programs to authorize ADS-B as primary surveillance in non-radar environments.
required_safety_constraints: ADS-B position depends on GPS — GPS outages or spoofing could degrade ADS-B position accuracy; ATC must have contingency procedures
ATC separation using ADS-B requires specific FAA LOA/authorization — not a default capability at all facilities
Pilots must have ADS-B Out (1090ES) per 14 CFR §91.225 to operate in Class A, Class B, and Class C airspace after January 1, 2020
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 90-114A — ADS-B operations
14 CFR §91.225 — ADS-B equipment requirements
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-2-23 — ADS-B separation services

provenance.generation_rule: ADS-B vs radar distinction from FAA AC 90-114A and JO 7110.65Z §5-2-23. ADS-B separation service authorization from FAA NextGen program documentation. Tests understanding of surveillance technology differences relevant to separation reliability.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is 'miles-in-trail' (MIT) restriction in ATC, and when is it applied?

If a facility issues a '20-in-trail' restriction, what does this require of departing or transitioning traffic?
gold_decision: Miles-in-trail (MIT) is a restriction requiring aircraft to maintain a specified distance (in nautical miles) behind the preceding aircraft on the same route or heading. MIT restrictions are issued by downstream facilities to manage arrival flow and traffic volume — they prevent saturation of arrival fixes or sectors. A '20-in-trail' restriction requires that successive aircraft on the restricted route/fix must be at least 20 NM apart from each other. MIT restrictions are flow-management tools — they are often issued as Traffic Management Initiatives (TMI) by the Air Traffic Control System Command Center (ATCSCC). Compliance is mandatory for the issuing and receiving facilities — departure times must be adjusted to meet the restriction.
required_safety_constraints: MIT is a Traffic Management Initiative — compliance is mandatory for facilities, not just a suggestion
MIT restrictions must be worked backwards: if aircraft depart too close together, MIT cannot be met at the downstream fix without vectoring or holding, creating downstream congestion
MIT does not replace separation minima — 5 NM radar separation must still be maintained in addition to the MIT spacing
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §17-2-3 — Miles-in-trail restrictions
FAA ATCSCC — Traffic Management Initiative procedures

provenance.generation_rule: Miles-in-trail definition from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §17-2-3. TMI compliance from ATCSCC procedures. Tests knowledge of flow-management restrictions that operate above and beyond standard separation minima.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Two aircraft are on crossing tracks converging toward a fix. Aircraft A is 12 NM from the fix at 450 kts groundspeed heading 090. Aircraft B is 15 NM from the fix at 300 kts groundspeed heading 000. Both are at FL350. Will they arrive at the fix simultaneously, and is there a separation issue?

Calculate the time to fix for each aircraft.
gold_decision: Aircraft A: 12 NM at 450 kts = 12/450 hours = 0.02667 hours = 1.6 minutes to the fix. Aircraft B: 15 NM at 300 kts = 15/300 hours = 0.05 hours = 3.0 minutes to the fix. Aircraft A arrives 1.4 minutes before Aircraft B. At FL350 in RVSM airspace, both at the same altitude (no vertical separation). Since Aircraft A arrives at the fix approximately 1.4 minutes before Aircraft B: when A is at the fix, B is 1.4 minutes × 300 kts = 1.4/60 × 300 = 7 NM from the fix. Horizontal separation at A's crossing time = approximately 7 NM. This exceeds the 5 NM en-route minimum. HOWEVER, the controller must verify separation throughout the approach to the fix, not just at the fix — the tracks converge and the minimum could be violated before or after the fix crossing depending on geometry.
required_safety_constraints: Separation must be verified at ALL points along converging tracks, not just at the intersection fix
7 NM at A's crossing time is above the 5 NM minimum but the controller must also check the minimum separation point which may be before the fix
Both aircraft at FL350 with no vertical separation means horizontal separation is the only protection — if it drops below 5 NM at any point, a safety alert is required
acceptable_variants: Exact minimum separation point requires full geometric calculation — 7 NM at the fix crossing is a necessary but not sufficient check
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-5-4 — En-route radar separation 5 NM
Time-to-fix calculation: distance/speed = time (NM / kt = hours; convert to minutes by × 60)

provenance.generation_rule: Crossing-track time-to-fix calculation. Aircraft A: 12 NM / 450 kt = 1.6 min. Aircraft B: 15 NM / 300 kt = 3.0 min. Difference 1.4 min. Separation at A's crossing = 1.4/60 × 300 = 7 NM. Both at FL350 = same altitude, no vertical separation. 7 NM > 5 NM minimum at crossing time, but geometry check is incomplete. Tests numerical separation calculation plus understanding that crossing-point check is necessary but not sufficient.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is the 'no transgression zone' (NTZ) in simultaneous independent parallel approach operations, and what must ATC do if an aircraft is observed entering the NTZ?

What is the required controller action and the time constraint?
gold_decision: The No Transgression Zone (NTZ) is a corridor 2,000 ft wide centered on the centerline between two parallel ILS approach courses during simultaneous independent parallel approaches. No aircraft may enter the NTZ — it is the safety buffer between the two parallel approach paths. If an aircraft is observed entering the NTZ: (1) The controller monitoring the NTZ must immediately issue a breakout instruction to the NTZ-violating aircraft ('Turn left/right heading XXX, climb to XXXXX') — this is a mandatory, non-negotiable action; (2) The other aircraft on the adjacent approach must also receive a breakout instruction if its safety is compromised; (3) The breakout instruction must be issued immediately — there is no time for deliberation. The entire NTZ response must be completed within approximately 15 seconds of the penetration being observed per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-7.
required_safety_constraints: NTZ penetration requires an IMMEDIATE breakout instruction — no deliberation is permitted
The NTZ monitor controller position is dedicated solely to NTZ monitoring — no other ATC functions during independent approaches
Once a breakout is issued, the other approach is also immediately broken off if there is any doubt about separation
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-7 — NTZ monitoring and breakout procedures
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-6 — Simultaneous independent approaches requirements

provenance.generation_rule: NTZ definition and breakout procedures from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-9-7. 2000 ft NTZ width from §5-9-6. 15-second response time from parallel approach operations training standards. Tests knowledge of the most time-critical ATC intervention in standard operations.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
Under ICAO Doc 4444, what are the four methods of establishing ATC separation?

List them in order from most to least commonly used in modern controlled airspace.
gold_decision: The four ICAO methods of establishing ATC separation are: (1) Surveillance separation (most common in modern airspace): using radar, ADS-B, or multilateration to track aircraft positions and maintain separation minima (3-5 NM horizontal, 1,000 ft vertical in RVSM); (2) Vertical separation: assigning different altitudes/flight levels — simplest and most reliable method; (3) Horizontal separation: placing aircraft on different geographic tracks (lateral) or ensuring longitudinal spacing (time/distance-based); (4) Time-based separation (least common in modern airspace, common in oceanic/remote): using position reports and estimated crossing times to ensure separation over reporting points. ICAO Doc 4444 §5.1 through §5.4 describes all four methods.
required_safety_constraints: Multiple methods may be used simultaneously — vertical + horizontal is common
In radar environments, surveillance is the primary method — vertical is the backup
Time-based methods have the largest uncertainty — position report accuracy determines safety margin
acceptable_variants: Some classification systems list only three primary methods (surveillance, vertical, horizontal) with time as a subset of horizontal — ICAO Doc 4444 §5 treats all four as separate
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 4444 §5.1 — Separation principles
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.3 — Vertical separation
ICAO Doc 4444 §5.4 — Horizontal separation (lateral and longitudinal)

provenance.generation_rule: Four separation methods from ICAO Doc 4444 §5.1-§5.4. Frequency ordering based on modern airspace practice (surveillance most common, time-based least common). Tests comprehensive knowledge of ATC separation methods framework.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
What is 'speed control' as a separation technique, and under what conditions can ATC require a pilot to reduce or increase speed for separation purposes?

Are there any airspace or altitude restrictions on speed control instructions?
gold_decision: Speed control is an ATC technique where controllers assign specific speeds or speed ranges to aircraft to maintain or establish in-trail separation. ATC may require speed adjustments for separation: (1) In terminal airspace below FL240, ATC may assign speeds down to 150 KIAS for jets and 120 KIAS for turboprops; (2) Speed assignments must be reasonable for the aircraft type; (3) Controllers cannot require pilots to exceed aircraft performance limits; (4) Speed control is particularly useful for sequencing arrivals without vectoring. Restrictions: MACH speed cannot be directly converted to KIAS without considering altitude (density altitude); in Class A airspace (FL180+), speeds are typically in Mach numbers. Pilots may deviate from speed assignments in emergencies per 14 CFR §91.3.
required_safety_constraints: Controllers must not assign speeds that exceed aircraft performance limits or endanger safety
Minimum speed assignments must account for the aircraft's stall speed with appropriate margins — the pilot has final authority if assigned speed is unsafe
Speed control for separation does NOT relieve the requirement to maintain horizontal/vertical separation minima — it is a supplementary technique
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-7-1 — Speed adjustment techniques
FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-7-2 — Speed limitations for separation

provenance.generation_rule: Speed control procedures from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §5-7-1 and §5-7-2. Minimum speed assignments from same sections. Tests knowledge of speed control as a separation tool and its limitations — relevant for agents reasoning about resolution advisory options.
provenance.license: SYNTHETIC — no copyright; constructed from ICAO Doc 4444 + FAA JO 7110.65 public rules

prompt:
When a pilot declares a MAYDAY (distress), what is the ATC-assigned transponder code, and what special ATC coordination is triggered?

How does a MAYDAY differ operationally from a PAN-PAN call from an ATC separation perspective?
gold_decision: MAYDAY uses transponder code 7700 (general emergency). Upon a MAYDAY: ATC triggers: (1) Alert to supervisor; (2) Alert adjacent facilities; (3) Maximum available assistance — clear airspace, coordinate emergency equipment, priority handling; (4) Notify appropriate facilities per FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-1. From separation: ATC must provide all other aircraft with separation from the MAYDAY aircraft AND provide the MAYDAY aircraft with priority routing/altitudes. PAN-PAN (urgency) is less severe — ATC provides priority handling but does not necessarily activate full emergency response; the aircraft may not squawk 7700 unless instructed. From separation perspective: MAYDAY triggers maximum airspace clearance (other aircraft moved away); PAN-PAN triggers preferential handling but not necessarily cleared airspace.
required_safety_constraints: 7700 enables radar identification enhancement — all facilities can see the emergency aircraft highlighted on radar
ATC must provide maximum assistance — this means vectors, altitudes, approaches, and airspace clearing
Both MAYDAY and PAN-PAN require supervisor notification — the difference is the urgency level and resource mobilization
acceptable_variants: Some international procedures use different squawk codes for distress vs urgency — in US airspace, 7700 is standard for both unless otherwise instructed
evidence_requirements: FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-1 — Emergency and distress procedures
ICAO Annex 10 Vol II §5.3.2 — Distress and urgency signals
14 CFR §91.3 — PIC emergency authority

provenance.generation_rule: MAYDAY procedures from FAA Order JO 7110.65Z §10-2-1. Squawk 7700 from ICAO Annex 10. PAN-PAN vs MAYDAY distinction from ICAO Doc 4444. Tests emergency procedures and ATC response — critical because agents must know that MAYDAY triggers mandatory separation actions, not just advisory ones.
